channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Historian Kerri K. Greenidge and Her Book ‘The Grimkes’ Come Under Fire
item.description: Kerri K. Greenidge appeared to lose her professorship at Tufts University after scholars began scrutinizing her 2022 book, “The Grimkes,” which is no longer listed on its publisher’s website.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.category: Black Radical: The Life and Times of William Monroe Trotter (Book)
item.media:credit: Mark Elzey Jr for The New York Times
item.media:description: Historians are questioning the validity of multiple works by the historian Kerri K. Greenidge, who is no longer employed by Tufts University.

item.title: From A.I. to the Deep State, Michel Foucault Foresaw It All
item.description: One hundred years after his birth, the French philosopher remains hugely influential, both revered and reviled for ideas that eerily anticipated our day.
item.dc:creator: Emily Eakin

item.title: 10 Kids’ Books for Fans of the Magic Tree House Series
item.description: Ten recommendations for fans of Mary Pope Osborne’s time-travel chapter book series.
item.dc:creator: Travis Jonker
item.category: The Story of Gumluck the Wizard (Book)

item.title: Alice Oseman on the End of ‘Heartstopper’ and Her Favorite Books
item.description: While the Y.A. graphic novel phenomenon wraps up with a sixth volume and a Netflix movie, Alice Oseman mostly reads fiction about adults now.

item.title: What to Read This Summer: NYT Reading Bucket List
item.description: Read along with the Book Review this summer: Can you check off five items before fall arrives? (This year, there are prizes!)
item.dc:creator: The New York Times Books Staff

item.title: Book Review: ‘Fixer Chao,’ by Han Ong
item.description: Han Ong’s barbed, irreverent portrait of identity, class envy and flimflam deception in turn-of-the-21st-century Manhattan still resonates.
item.dc:creator: Leah Greenblatt

item.title: Hershel Parker, Melville Scholar of Ahab-Like Obsessiveness, Dies at 90
item.description: The author of an exhaustive two-volume biography, he was “fanatical about his approach to scholarship and infatuated with Melville,” a colleague said.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: Dinaw Mengestu Resigns as PEN America President After 7 Months in Role
item.description: The novelist Dinaw Mengestu, who was elected seven months ago, said the article “continues this approach toward defending some rights while not defending others.”
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez
item.media:description: Dinaw Mengestu had been on PEN America’s board for about a decade before he became its president in December.

item.title: This Author Says You Shouldn’t Be Intimidated by ‘The Odyssey’
item.description: First, a primer from the author of “Circe,” Madeline Miller. Then, A.O. Scott on all the genres inside the 3,000-year-old poem.
item.dc:creator: Gilbert Cruz, Sarah Diamond, Amy Pearl, Patricia Sulbarán and Tracy Mumford
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times; Madeline Miller: Christopher Leaman for The New York Times

item.title: Book Review: ‘A Vast Horizon,’ by Anna Thomasson
item.description: A group of artists gathered at a hotel on the Côte d’Azur in 1937. A new book by Anna Thomasson captures the art and escapades the holiday inspired.
item.dc:creator: Emily Eakin
item.category: A Vast Horizon: Artists and Lovers, Freedom and War (Book)
item.media:description: Pablo Picasso and Dora Maar at Golfe-Juan, France, on the Côte d’Azur in 1937.

item.title: Books Our Editors Love This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times
item.category: American Trickster: The Hidden Lives of Carlos Castaneda (Book)

item.title: Too Many Books?
item.description: Mendel Uminer faced a crisis when his landlord objected to the 10,000 volumes in his New York studio apartment.
item.dc:creator: Alex Vadukul
item.media:description: “I don’t think of myself as a hoarder,” Mendel Uminer said, “but I guess my building did.”

item.title: Homer’s Epic ‘Odyssey’ Has Something for Every Reader, From Sci-Fi to Romantasy
item.description: The world’s most famous epic has something for every reader.
item.dc:creator: A.O. Scott

item.title: Which Version of the ‘Odyssey’ Should You Read?
item.description: Homer’s “Odyssey” has been translated into English countless times, with versions ranging from contemporary and accessible to highly poetic. A.O. Scott, critic at large for The New York Times Book Review, breaks down three translations and explains which one might be right for you.
item.dc:creator: A.O. Scott, Léo Hamelin, Gabriel Blanco, Laura Salaberry and Lauren Pruitt

item.title: Historical Fiction Books That Will Transport You to the 1960s
item.description: The best-selling author Kerri Maher recommends books that transport readers to the decade of space travel, counterculture and tumultuous societal change.
item.dc:creator: Kerri Maher
item.media:credit: Image by The New York Times; Author photo by Kate Eden Renyi Photography

item.title: A New ‘Little House’ Broadens and Updates the Prairie
item.description: A Netflix adaptation of the beloved tale empowers Ma and adds new Black and Native characters to challenge prejudices. The creators know criticism is coming.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.category: Little House on the Prairie (2026) (TV Program)
Little House on the Prairie (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Karen Asher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Netflix’s “Little House on the Prairie” was filmed in Winnipeg. The new Ingalls family includes Skywalker Hughes as Mary, left, and Alice Halsey as Laura.

item.title: Book Review: ‘A Table for Fortune,’ by William T. Vollmann
item.description: William T. Vollmann’s fascinating, wildly ambitious text interweaves a family saga with a history of the American war machine since Vietnam.
item.dc:creator: Tom LeClair

item.title: Hachette Book Group Employees Vote to Unionize
item.description: Workers at a major publishing house have established what organizers are calling the largest union in trade book publishing history.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: Book Review: ‘City of Fortune,’ by Mason B. Williams
item.description: A new book by the historian Mason B. Williams chronicles the dubious effects of school, housing and police reforms by local politicians on the right.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Heilbrunn
item.category: City of Fortune: Inequality and the Making of Contemporary New York (Book)
item.media:description: Rather than solving the city’s woes, neoconservatives, including Rudy Giuliani, created new ones, Williams argues.

item.title: Book Review: ‘You Won’t Get Free of It,’ by Rachel Aviv
item.description: Rachel Aviv’s essay collection, “You Won’t Get Free of It,” shows the breadth and complexity of matrilineal relationships.
item.dc:creator: Melanie Thernstrom
item.category: You Won't Get Free of It: Stories of Mothers and Daughters (Book)

item.title: Want to Truly Understand the World Cup? Check Out These Books.
item.description: Three authors chronicle the history of the World Cup and of U.S. men’s soccer.
item.dc:creator: Miguel Salazar
item.category: The Power and the Glory: The History of the World Cup (Book)
World Cup Fever: A Soccer Journey in Nine Tournaments (Book)
The Long Game: U.S. Mens Soccer and Its Savage, Four-Decade Journey to the Top, or Thereabouts (Book)
item.media:description: Folarin Balogun during last week’s World Cup match in which the United States defeated Bosnia and Herzegovina. He scored the Americans’ first goal.

item.title: Understanding the World Cup in 3 Books
item.description: Miguel Salazar of the New York Times Books Review recommends three titles that can help you understand the history of the World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Miguel Salazar and Haimy Assefa

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Coast of Everything,’ by Guillermo Stitch
item.description: “The Coast of Everything,” by Guillermo Stitch, is structured like a nesting doll, with one story hidden within another.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Illingworth
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; photograph by Sarah Blesener for The New York Times

item.title: Book Review: ‘Men in Love,’ by Irvine Welsh
item.description: Irvine Welsh brings back his beloved Edinburgh ensemble for his new novel, “Men in Love.”
item.dc:creator: James Lasdun

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Simp,’ by Roshan Sethi
item.description: “The Simp,” by Roshan Sethi, is a satire of the unending demands, learned helplessness and stubborn racism of Hollywood elites.
item.dc:creator: Sam Lipsyte

item.title: Mike Wallace, Who Wrote an Acclaimed History of New York, Dies at 83
item.description: His 1998 book, “Gotham,” which told the city’s story to 1898, focused on social and economic conflict. It won a Pulitzer Prize and inspired two sequels.
item.dc:creator: Sam Roberts
item.category: Greater Gotham: A History of New York City from 1898 to 1919 (Book)
Gotham: A History of New York City to 1898 (Book)
Gotham at War: A History of New York City from 1933 to 1945 (Book)
item.media:credit: Elizabeth D. Herman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mike Wallace in 2017. The Pulitzer committee said that “Gotham,” which he wrote with Edwin G. Burrows, tied many strands “into a single narrative tapestry that reads like a fast-paced novel.”

item.title: ‘The Face,’ by Fay Bound-Alberti
item.description: In “The Face,” Fay Bound-Alberti examines our relationship to our most public features.
item.dc:creator: Ginia Bellafante
item.media:credit: Fred R. Conrad/The New York Times
item.media:description: Brad Pitt, like the author, is affected by prosopagnosia, or face blindness.

item.title: Book Review: ‘Country People,’ by Daniel Mason
item.description: In Daniel Mason’s charming “Country People,” a stuck father and his happier family are tempted, and changed, during a stint on a bucolic campus.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: Book Review: ‘American Trickster,’ by Ru Marshall
item.description: In “American Trickster,” Ru Marshall takes on the epic story of Carlos Castaneda.
item.dc:creator: Kurt Andersen
item.category: American Trickster: The Hidden Lives of Carlos Castaneda (Book)
